target: Cluster-wide Chapman/Kim&Chang/Yamazaki&Maute citation canonicalization across WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068. Single coordinated patch resolving ISS-1287, ISS-1308, ISS-1441, ISS-1491, ISS-1492 (Chapman-citation family) and one newly-discovered WIN-068 citation error (DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 does NOT belong to Yamazaki & Maute — it resolves to Meza et al. 2021 Patagonian Solar Eclipse paper; correct Yamazaki & Maute citation is 2017 Space Science Reviews 206:299–405 DOI 10.1007/s11214-016-0282-z). Also corrects Kim & Chang page range from 2040–2054 to 2040–2049 (ADS bibcode 2018AdSpR..61.2040K, publisher-confirmed lastpage = 2049).

citation_verification_summary.Chapman_1919: VERIFIED. Chapman, S. (1919). 'The solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism.' Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. A 218:1–118. DOI 10.1098/rsta.1919.0001. Foundational Sq-theory paper. Royal Society Publishing record confirmed.
citation_verification_summary.Chapman_1933: DEPRECATED from eclipse-Sq citations. Chapman 1933 Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3 is about LUNAR geomagnetic tides, not solar-eclipse Sq (per curmudgeon c13 citation_check). Currently standing in WIN-010 finding/tldr_evidence/tldr_verdict/detail_evidence/detail_verdict_text and WIN-025 finding/tldr_evidence/tldr_verdict/detail_evidence/detail_verdict_text. Replace with Chapman 1919.
citation_verification_summary.Kim_Chang_2018: VERIFIED but PAGE RANGE INCORRECT. Kim, J.-H. & Chang, H.-Y. (2018). 'Statistical analysis of geomagnetic field variations during solar eclipses.' Adv. Space Res. 61(8):2040–2049. DOI 10.1016/j.asr.2018.01.022. ADS bibcode 2018AdSpR..61.2040K confirms citation_lastpage = 2049. Current WIN text uses 61:2040–2054, which is wrong by 5 pages. Fix.
citation_verification_summary.Yamazaki_Maute_2017: VERIFIED as the correct citation. Yamazaki, Y. & Maute, A. (2017). 'Sq and EEJ—A Review on the Daily Variation of the Geomagnetic Field Caused by Ionospheric Dynamo Currents.' Space Science Reviews 206(1):299–405. DOI 10.1007/s11214-016-0282-z. Comprehensive review of Sq/ionospheric conductivity, open-access. This is the canonical modern review cited as 'modern conductivity treatment' across the cluster.
citation_verification_summary.Yamazaki_Maute_2021_ERROR: INCORRECT CITATION DETECTED. The string 'Yamazaki & Maute (2021) Front. Astron. Space Sci. DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327' appears in WIN-068 finding, detail_evidence, and detail_verdict_text. DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 actually resolves to: Meza, A., Eylenstein, B., Natali, M. P. et al. (2021) 'Analysis of Ionospheric and Geomagnetic Response to the 2020 Patagonian Solar Eclipse,' Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences. Different paper entirely. Fix by replacing with correct Yamazaki & Maute (2017) citation in all three WIN-068 fields. (This was flagged by curmudgeon c13 citation_check as 'citations_verified' — curmudgeon did not catch the DOI mismatch. Noted in prior-citation-check-correction below.)
citation_verification_summary.Rastogi_1962: UNVERIFIABLE — citation dropped. Curmudgeon c13 stronger_arguments flagged: 'Rastogi 1962 citation in the Finding is a family reference rather than a specific paper.' Could not locate a specific Rastogi 1962 eclipse-Sq paper via ADS/web search. Rastogi published 'Longitudinal variation in the equatorial electrojet' in J. Atmos. Terr. Phys. (1962) — but that is not eclipse-specific. Rather than propagate an ambiguous citation cluster-wide, drop Rastogi from WIN-068 (the only WIN carrying it) and lean on the already-strong Chapman 1919 + Yamazaki & Maute 2017 + Kim & Chang 2018 trio. This tightens the citation set, not weakens it.

prior_citation_check_correction: The curmudgeon c13 citation_check marked 'Yamazaki & Maute (2021) Front. Astron. Space Sci. DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — real paper and open-access URL verifiable' as VERIFIED. That is incorrect: the DOI resolves to Meza et al. (2021), not Yamazaki & Maute. This EXP corrects the citation to Yamazaki & Maute (2017) Space Science Reviews 206:299–405 DOI 10.1007/s11214-016-0282-z, which is the real modern Sq review the WIN-068 text intends to invoke. Curmudgeon c14 will see the correction and can re-mark.

patches.after: The Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework, documented since Chapman (1919) and quantitatively confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang (2018)

patches.rationale: Rewrite the Chapman-introduction sentence to cite Chapman (1919) with full venue (Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118) and fold in Yamazaki & Maute (2017) as the modern conductivity treatment. Removes the mis-attributed 1933 reference.
patches.before: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first described by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering conductivity and altering the Sq current system. Chapman's original work was semi-quantitative; detailed magnitude predictions came from later studies and modern ionospheric models.
patches.after: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations follow directly from the Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework, first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118) to describe the solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism. During an eclipse, the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq current system; the comprehensive modern review of Sq conductivity is Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405). Chapman's framework was semi-quantitative at origin; detailed magnitude predictions come from the modern Sq literature and ionospheric models.

patches.rationale: Two fixes in one substitution: (1) replace Chapman 1933 Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. with Chapman 1919 Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118 and insert Yamazaki & Maute 2017 Space Science Reviews 206:299–405 as modern conductivity treatment; (2) correct Kim & Chang 2018 page range from 61:2040–2054 to 61:2040–2049 (ADS bibcode 2018AdSpR..61.2040K confirms lastpage = 2049).
patches.before: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054)
patches.after: The Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework — first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118), with modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405) — and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2049)

Replace '1933 — 93 years before the dome claimed it' with '1919 — 107 years before the dome claimed it'. Both figures are computed from 2026 minus the Chapman year: 2026 − 1933 = 93, 2026 − 1919 = 107. Canonicalizing on Chapman 1919 naturally updates the 'years before the dome claimed it' figure.

anticipated_objections.objection: Is replacing Chapman 1933 with Chapman 1919 substantive, or just pedantry?
anticipated_objections.response: Substantive. Chapman 1933 (Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) is a LUNAR diurnal geomagnetic tide paper, not the founding Sq-framework paper. Chapman 1919 (Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118) IS the founding Sq paper — he introduces solar and lunar diurnal variations and gives the dynamo-current framework the rest of the century built on. A reader who follows 'Chapman 1933' expecting to find the Sq-theory foundation will find a different paper. The pedantry charge fails because the citation targets different physics.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why drop Rastogi 1962 — isn't keeping more citations better?
anticipated_objections.response: More verified citations are better; ambiguous citations are worse than no citation. Curmudgeon c13 stronger_arguments #1 flagged that 'Rastogi 1962' is a family reference. We could not locate a specific Rastogi 1962 paper that anchors the eclipse-Sq application (his 1962 J. Atmos. Terr. Phys. paper is on longitudinal electrojet variation, not eclipse-Sq). Rather than propagate an unverifiable citation cluster-wide, drop it and rely on Chapman 1919 + Yamazaki & Maute 2017 + Kim & Chang 2018. This is a TIGHTER citation set, not weaker. If a defender ever challenges a Rastogi cite we cannot produce, we lose credibility; if a defender challenges the absence of Rastogi, we say 'the Sq framework is sufficiently anchored by Chapman 1919 and the modern Y&M 2017 review.'

anticipated_objections.objection: The curmudgeon c13 verified the Yamazaki & Maute 2021 citation — are we sure it's wrong?
anticipated_objections.response: Yes. Direct verification: fetching https://doi.org/10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 returns a Frontiers page whose citation_title meta is 'Analysis of Ionospheric and Geomagnetic Response to the 2020 Patagonian Solar Eclipse' and citation_author is 'Meza, Amalia; Eylenstein, Bernardo; Natali, María Paula...' — NOT Yamazaki & Maute. The curmudgeon's citation_check must have only validated that the DOI resolved (HTTP 200) rather than that the resolved paper matched the claimed author-and-year. The correct Yamazaki & Maute paper intended by the WIN-068 prose (a comprehensive Sq/EEJ review) is their 2017 Space Science Reviews paper, DOI 10.1007/s11214-016-0282-z, Volume 206(1):299–405, verified via Springer metadata.

anticipated_objections.objection: Kim & Chang pages 2054 vs 2049 — really matters?
anticipated_objections.response: Yes for citation integrity. A reader who follows the page range 2040–2054 and finds the paper ends at 2049 will reasonably wonder what the extra five pages were padded with and whether our reading of the paper is accurate. A 5-page discrepancy in a 9-page paper (the paper is 10 pages, 2040–2049) is > 50% overstatement of the page range. Fix once, cluster-wide, while we are canonicalizing.

kernel_of_truth: The Chapman 1933 Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. paper IS real and Chapman DID publish work on geomagnetic diurnal variations in 1933. A defender could reasonably say 'Chapman worked on this in 1933, and the citation is not fabricated.' That is true at the authorship level. What the original WIN-010/025 prose intended, however, is the FOUNDING Sq-framework paper, and that is the 1919 Phil. Trans. A paper, not the 1933 lunar-tide paper. Similarly, Yamazaki & Maute have real co-authored work (multiple papers across 2015–2021), so 'Yamazaki & Maute 2021' is plausible at the authorship level; it just happens that the specific DOI the WIN-068 prose used belongs to Meza et al., not Y&M.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_the_dome: This expansion doesn't patch a dome claim — it corrects our own review's citations. The dome is not 'saved' by anything here; if anything, tightening our citations strengthens our position, because defender attacks at the citation level get harder.
cross_win_consistency_note: After this patch: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all cite the same canonical trio: Chapman 1919 (founding Sq), Yamazaki & Maute 2017 (modern conductivity review), Kim & Chang 2018 (39-eclipse ensemble). No residual Chapman 1933 references in the Sq context. No residual Yamazaki & Maute 2021 references. No Rastogi 1962 (dropped as unverifiable eclipse-specific citation). Kim & Chang page range consistent at 2040–2049 cluster-wide.
out_of_scope_deliberately: Triple-counting / cluster-inflation argument — only the cluster-note citation is canonicalized (W010-DV2); the substance of the argument is unchanged.
Chernogor & Holub 2024 citation in WIN-025 — already DOI-verified and unaffected.
tldr_propagation: No section-level TLDR updates needed. Each WIN's tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict are included in the per-field patches where their prose changes (W010-TE1/TV1, W025-TE1/TV1). Arguments and thrust of each section are preserved; only the citation year and derivative age-arithmetic change.
next_cycle_expectation: WIN-068 c14 review (next cycle) should see all Chapman-year/Yamazaki-year/Kim-Chang-pages holes closed. The 5 tracked issues (ISS-1287, 1308, 1441, 1491, 1492) should close together. WIN-010 and WIN-025 next curmudgeon cycles will see citation-canonicalization propagation accepted. If c14 re-opens a sixth Chapman issue, the problem is something new rather than piecemeal residual.
